Complete Column D for every row. For rows asking for years of experience, enter the number of years and include a brief note on scope or relevant clients. For all other rows, provide a written response. Specific, evidence-based answers are expected.

Complete Column D for every row. For rows asking for years of experience, enter the number of years and include a brief note on scope or relevant clients. For all other rows, provide a written response. Specific, evidence-based answers are expected.
Track Record — Financial Services Issuer Services

Describe your organization's overall track record serving card-issuing financial institutions. Include years in market, number of active issuer clients, approximate ACV managed, and any notable performance milestones.

Cognizant has built a focused, specialized Cards & Payments practice over the past 29 years as a grounds-up domain practice purpose-built for the integrated operational, regulatory, and technology requirements of issuers, processors, issuer-processors, aggregators, consolidators and digital banks. Our cards programs have been highly successful in client-adoption backed up with multi-year tenured relationships, multi-tower scope, high volume of transactions processed, all backed up with rigorous SLA, security, and regulatory compliance frameworks. This section describes programs in active delivery mode today.

Organizational Credentials
● Years in Cards & Payments:  29+ years of continuous, domain-focused delivery
● Cards & Payments Clients Served:  125+ globally, spanning issuers, processors, networks, and fintech's
● Dedicated C&P Workforce:  12,000+ professionals across BPO delivery, technology, analytics, and domain consulting
● Enterprise Associate Base:  351,600+ associates across 37+ countries (publicly reported, NASDAQ: CTSH)
● Global Delivery Centers:  40+ company-owned centers across four continents
● BPO Practice Client Base:  230+ clients across multiple business verticals

Scope of Active Delivery Across Issuer Clients
Across our active issuer client base, Cognizant delivers the following functions under long-term, outcome-driven managed services contracts:
● Customer servicing, IVR optimization, and live agent operations for consumer reach-out for due diligence, consumer credit, commercial, debit, prepaid, loyalty, healthcare, and retail card programs
● End-to-end disputes, chargebacks, and fraud monitoring including case management, regulatory-compliant resolution workflows, and investigative operations
● Card back-office processing: imaging, document management, GL balancing, settlement, reconciliation, payment research, and account maintenance
● Payments processing support across real-time and batch environments
● Loyalty and rewards program servicing including redemption handling, tier management, and partner fulfilment

Notable Performance Milestones
● Fraud Operations Efficiency: An average of 30% in efficiency improvement achieved across active issuer programs through automation, AI-assisted analytics, and digital workflow transformation.
● Disputes & Chargebacks at Scale: End-to-end dispute and chargeback operations delivered for leading card issuers across multiple jurisdictions under strict SLA and regulatory compliance frameworks.
● Transaction Processing Volume: Over $9 Billion in card and payment transactions processed through large-scale modernization programs for issuer and issuer-processor clients.
● Program Longevity: Issuer relationships characterized by multi-year tenures sustained through platform migrations, regulatory change, and business growth not point-in-time engagements.

Approximate ACV figures are commercially sensitive and are not shared due to client confidentiality, consistent with our obligations as a U.S. listed company.

Describe your approach to managing sub-contractors used in delivering TMS services. Confirm: (1) whether sub-contractors would be used on this engagement, which subcontractors would be used and where work would be performed; (2) your SLA flow-down mechanism; (3) whether the client retains audit rights over sub-contractors.

Cognizant's delivery model for this TMS engagement is fully first-party. We do not propose to use sub-contractors for any primary or secondary service delivery across any of the seven proposed delivery locations. Every associate delivering on this engagement will be a direct Cognizant employee, onboarded under Cognizant's global HR, security vetting, and compliance frameworks.

(1)  Sub-Contractor Usage on This Engagement
No sub-contractors will be used. The suggested delivery sites in San Salvador (El Salvador), Manila (Philippines), and Coimbatore (India) are wholly Cognizant-owned, operated, and governed facilities. There are no third-party staffing agencies, sub-contracted delivery centers, or outsourced specialist functions within the scope of this proposal. 
(2)  SLA Flow-Down Mechanism
All applicable service levels, performance metrics, security, regulatory, and compliance obligations would be contractually flowed down to any approved subcontractor. Cognizant would retain end to end accountability for SLA performance
(3)  FIS Audit Rights Over Sub-Contractors
YES confirmed. FIS retains full audit rights over any sub-contractor engaged on this engagement.

How many years has your organization delivered end-to-end risk mitigation covering fraud, collections, disputes, and credit bureau management. Report the number of years your organization has continuously operated this function. Do not sum individual staff years across people — report the longest unbroken period your organization has actively delivered this service.

15+ years  Cognizant has continuously delivered end to end risk mitigation services for over 15 years as an enterprise level capability. This represents the longest unbroken period during which Cognizant has actively operated this function, and is not an aggregation of individual staff experience.
Scope covered throughout this period includes:
• Fraud Mitigation and Investigation services span the full fraud lifecycle—alerting, investigation, decisioning, and recovery—supported by a scaled delivery model of approximately 1,900 dedicated fraud FTEs, enabling high‑volume processing, timely resolution, and consistent risk outcomes across operations.
• Disputes and claims management (chargebacks, representment, arbitration)
• Collections related loss prevention and recovery activities
• Credit bureau–linked processes (data validation, reporting, compliance aligned workflows)
This continuous delivery is documented across Cognizant’s Banking & Payments and Fraud & Disputes Operations capability materials, which explicitly state “15+ years of experience” delivering these services at scale for financial institutions

How many years has your organization delivered customer care for card programs (commercial, consumer credit, debit, retail, loyalty, healthcare, prepaid). Scope includes: IVR and live agent handling, multilingual and after-hours support, lost/stolen servicing, and loyalty programs. Report the number of years your organization has continuously operated this function. Do not sum individual staff years across people — report the longest unbroken period your organization has actively delivered this service.

15+ Years   longest unbroken period of continuous, organizational-level card customer care delivery

This figure represents the longest unbroken period during which Cognizant has actively operated end-to-end card customer care services at an organizational level. It is not an aggregation of individual staff tenure or team-level experience. It reflects institutional capability built and sustained through program evolution, platform migrations, channel transformation, and regulatory change.

Scope of Continuous Delivery Throughout This Period
• IVR Design & Live Agent Operations: Delivered across consumer credit, commercial, debit, prepaid, healthcare, retail, and loyalty card programs, including skill-based routing, AI-assisted agent enablement, and real-time escalation management.
• Multilingual Support: 30+ languages supported across a globally distributed delivery network, with 24/7/365 coverage across time zones and geographies.
• After-Hours & Weekend Coverage: Consistent SLA adherence through follow-the-sun delivery models and dedicated after-hours teams embedded within program structures.
• Lost & Stolen Card Servicing: Real-time card blocking, fraud-linked escalation, reissuance coordination, and cardholder communication, integrated with issuer fraud operations and regulatory reporting requirements.
• Loyalty Program Servicing: Redemption handling, account queries, partner fulfilment, tier management, and promotional event support for major issuer loyalty programs.

Continuity Enablers
• Standardized Operating Procedures: Consistent, auditable SOPs across all program tiers enabling rapid onboarding, quality assurance, and regulatory readiness independent of personnel changes.
• Workforce Management Discipline: Demand forecasting, schedule optimization, and capacity planning frameworks that have maintained SLA performance through volume spikes and seasonal patterns.
• Regulatory Continuity: Compliance frameworks updated proactively through regulatory change across PCI DSS, CFPB, GDPR, and regional card-specific requirements without service interruption.
• Technology Agnosticism: Delivery sustained across multiple card platform generations and channel evolution without program disruption.

How many years has your organization delivered back-office processing for card programs. Scope includes: imaging, correspondence processing, payment research, balance transfers, credit balance refunds, account maintenance, general ledger (GL) balancing and settlement, reconciliation of GL accounts, and returned mail processing. Note: application processing is not in scope for this engagement. Report the number of years your organization has continuously operated this function. Do not sum individual staff years across people — report the longest unbroken period your organization has actively delivered this service.

15+ Years   longest unbroken period of continuous, enterprise-level card back-office processing delivery

This figure represents the longest unbroken period during which Cognizant has actively operated card back-office processing services at scale for card issuers and issuer-processors. Application processing is excluded from scope, consistent with the RFP definition. This is an organizational-level figure and does not aggregate individual staff tenure.
In-Scope Functions Delivered Continuously for 15+ Years
• Imaging & Document Management: High-volume document capture, classification, indexing, and archival with audit-grade retrieval capabilities and retention controls.
• Correspondence Processing: Inbound and outbound correspondence management across print, digital, and hybrid channels with SLA-governed turnaround times and exception handling.
• Payment Research & Resolution: Investigation and resolution of payment discrepancies, misapplied payments, and exception items across consumer and commercial portfolios.
• Balance Transfers & Credit Balance Refunds: End-to-end processing of balance transfer requests and credit balance refund disbursements with accuracy controls and full audit trails.
• Account Maintenance: Full-lifecycle account maintenance across consumer and commercial card portfolios including address changes, product conversions, limit modifications, and account status updates.
• GL Balancing & Settlement: Daily general ledger balancing, settlement processing, and exception management with same-day resolution targets and escalation protocols.
• Reconciliation of GL Accounts: Structured reconciliation cycles with maker-checker controls, variance reporting, and escalation frameworks ensuring financial accuracy and audit readiness.
• Returned Mail Processing: Undeliverable mail handling, address verification, NCOA-integrated resolution workflows, and cardholder notification management.

Control & Governance Architecture
• Segregation of Duties: Maker-checker controls embedded across all financial processing workflows, preventing single-point errors and fraud exposure.
• Reconciliation Integrity: Structured exception handling with audit-ready documentation at every reconciliation cycle and variance escalation within agreed thresholds.
• Regulatory Compliance: Operations mapped to applicable financial services regulatory requirements across all active geographies, updated proactively through regulatory change.
• Operational Continuity: Scalable delivery architecture engineered for consistent processing through daily settlement cycles, volume peaks, and business growth without service degradation.
Years of Experience — Client Operations Oversight

How many years has your organization delivered structured client-facing operational support. Scope includes: day-to-day client inquiry and escalation management, monthly and quarterly business reviews, and SLA monitoring. Report the number of years your organization has continuously operated this function. Do not sum individual staff years across people — report the longest unbroken period your organization has actively delivered this service.

15+  Years longest unbroken period of continuous, enterprise-level structured client-facing operational support

Structured client governance is not a support function at Cognizant it is a delivery discipline embedded from program inception. For 15 consecutive years, Cognizant has operated formal, multi-tiered client-facing governance frameworks across its managed services portfolio, including Cards & Payments engagements of the scale and complexity relevant to FIS. This is an organizational-level figure and does not aggregate individual staff tenure.

Scope of Continuous Delivery Throughout This Period
• Day-to-Day Client Inquiry Management: Defined response timelines, ownership protocols, and escalation paths for all client queries governed at engagement level, not handled ad hoc.
• Escalation Management: Structured escalation tiers from operational team lead through to executive sponsor, with committed response and resolution timeframes embedded in the service agreement.
• Monthly Business Reviews (MBRs): Standardized reporting packs covering SLA performance, volume trends, quality metrics, risk items, continuous improvement status, and forward-looking actions.
• Quarterly Business Reviews (QBRs): Executive-level strategic reviews covering program health, capacity planning, transformation initiatives, regulatory developments, and relationship alignment.
• SLA & KPI Monitoring: Real-time performance dashboards with threshold alerting, trend analysis, and proactive exception reporting not retrospective reporting cycles.

Proposed Governance Cadence for the FIS Engagement
• Weekly Operational Review: program Manager and Operations Lead SLA scorecard, exception items, and near-term risk.
• Monthly Business Review: Delivery Director and Client Service Executive full SLA scorecard, quality trends, workforce metrics, and improvement actions.
• Quarterly Business Review: VP and Executive Sponsor program health, strategic alignment, transformation roadmap, and commercial performance.
• Semi-Annual Executive Session: Senior VP and C-Suite strategic relationship health, forward-looking investment priorities, and innovation pipeline.
• Real-Time SLA Dashboard: Shared portal giving FIS continuous visibility to live performance data at any point, not waiting for review cycles.
Number of Active Financial Services Clients
How many active financial services clients does your organization currently serve? Break out by client type (issuer, acquirer, payments processor, other) where possible.

As a publicly listed company (NASDAQ: CTSH), Cognizant is restricted from disclosing active client counts and client-type distributions that are not part of our public financial reporting. We provide the most complete permissible response below and commit to supplementing with further detail under NDA during commercial negotiations.
What We Can Share Publicly
● BPO Practice: Total Clients Served:  230+ clients across multiple business verticals, served from 40+ global delivery centers
● Cards & Payments: Global Clients:  125+ clients across the full C&P ecosystem
● Enterprise Footprint:  351,600+ associates delivering services across 37+ countries

Within the Cards & Payments domain, Cognizant's client base spans the full payments ecosystem: card issuers (consumer and commercial), merchant acquirers, issuer-processors, global payment networks, and fintech challengers. The 125+ figure represents clients served through Cognizant's global Cards & Payments practice over time. The current active client count and client-type breakdown are commercially sensitive and available for discussion under NDA at advanced stages of this procurement.

Reference Access
Cognizant's Cards & Payments client base includes organizations structurally comparable to FIS in scale, complexity, and regulatory environment — including large issuer-processors, top-tier US and European card issuers, and global payment networks. We are prepared to provide redacted reference narratives or facilitate structured reference calls under appropriate NDA arrangements.

Which languages do you currently support across your delivery network? List associated delivery locations. Of particular relevance: English (US, CA, UK), Spanish (US), French-Canadian, German, Dutch, Portuguese.

Cognizant supports 30+ languages across its globally distributed, company-owned delivery network. All seven priority languages identified by FIS are in active delivery today. Language capabilities are delivered using native and near-native speakers, reinforced by dedicated Language Centers of Excellence, CEFR-aligned proficiency assessments, and continuous training and certification programs.

Priority Languages for the FIS Engagement
• English (US): United States (Des Moines, Scranton, Plano, Tampa, Phoenix / Mesa), India (multiple locations), Philippines (Manila, Cebu)
• English (UK): United Kingdom (multiple sites), Ireland (Dublin), India
• English (Canada): Canada (Toronto, Halifax), India
• Spanish (US): United States (multiple sites), Mexico (Mexico City), El Salvador (San Salvador), Argentina (Buenos Aires), Philippines
• French-Canadian: Canada (Toronto, Halifax), India — delivered via Cognizant's French Language Centre of Excellence
• German: Germany (Hamburg), Poland (Gdansk, Krakow), Hungary (Budapest), India
• Dutch: Poland (Gdansk, Krakow), Portugal (Lisbon), Ireland (Dublin)
• Portuguese: Brazil (Sao Paulo), Portugal (Lisbon), Argentina (Buenos Aires), India

Additional Language Coverage
Beyond the priority languages above, Cognizant actively delivers services in the following languages, all available for FIS program scope expansion without requiring new site build-out: French (European), Italian, Polish, Swedish, Norwegian, Finnish, Hungarian, Mandarin, Cantonese, Japanese, Korean, Thai, Vietnamese, Indonesian, Arabic, Malay, and Tagalog.

Language Quality Assurance
• CEFR-Aligned Assessment: All language hires are assessed against the Common European Framework of Reference before deployment on client programs.
• Language Centers of Excellence: Dedicated CoEs for high-volume languages — English, Spanish, French, German, and Portuguese — providing ongoing coaching, quality monitoring, and proficiency certification.
• Bilingual Quality Monitoring: Quality assurance and calibration conducted by native or near-native speakers for all non-English language streams.
• Accent Neutralization Training: Structured programs for client-facing roles where accent neutrality is a program requirement.

What is your total headcount across all geographies? Break out by: (1) front-office agent FTE; (2) back-office agent FTE; (3) support FTE (QA, WFM, Training, Management, Analytics); (4) approximate onshore / nearshore / offshore split (%).

As a publicly listed company (NASDAQ: CTSH), Cognizant does not disclose disaggregated workforce data  including FTE breakdowns by functional category or detailed delivery geography splits as this constitutes commercially sensitive information that could affect competitive positioning and client confidentiality obligations.

Enterprise-Level Scale Indicators
● Total Enterprise Associates:  351,600+ across 37+ countries (publicly reported)
● Cards & Payments Professionals:  12,000+ dedicated practitioners across BPO delivery, technology, analytics, and domain consulting
● Global Delivery Centers:  40+ company-owned centers spanning all major delivery geographies
● BPO Practice:  230+ clients served across multiple verticals

What Cognizant Will Provide for the FIS Engagement
Within the formal commercial proposal and staffing plan, Cognizant will provide the following specifics scoped precisely to the agreed service towers and contracted volumes:
• Front-Office Agent FTE: By site, language stream, and service tower — with full ramp schedule and steady-state staffing model.
• Back-Office Processing FTE: By function (imaging, correspondence, GL, reconciliation, etc.) and delivery geography.
• Support Staff FTE: Detailed breakdown across QA, WFM, Training, Management, and Analytics roles with span-of-control ratios.
• Delivery Geography Split: Precise onshore, nearshore, and offshore allocation by service tower with commercial rationale and regulatory considerations.
• Surge Capacity Model: Flex workforce planning for seasonal peaks, volume growth scenarios, and business continuity requirements.

Cognizant is committed to providing full FTE modelling, ramp schedules, and geo-split rationale as part of our commercial submission and to standing behind those numbers with contractual headcount commitments and SLA-linked staffing floors.
What were your voluntary agent attrition rates for each of the last three calendar years? Break out by geography where materially different.

As a publicly listed company (NASDAQ: CTSH), Cognizant does not disclose geography-specific or function-specific attrition data, as this is not part of our public financial reporting and constitutes commercially sensitive workforce information. We share firm-wide annualized attrition figures published as part of our investor communications.

Enterprise-Wide Voluntary Attrition
● 2023:  13.8% annualized
● 2024:  15.9% annualized
● 2025:  13.9% annualized

Why Dedicated program Attrition Differs from Enterprise Rates
• Dedicated Account Structures: Associates in single-client programs develop deep institutional knowledge, clear career progression paths, and strong program identity all proven attrition inhibitors.
• Tenure-Linked Incentive Structures: Compensation and recognition frameworks designed to reward tenure and performance on the specific account, not general market mobility.
• Career Architecture: program-level career laddering from agent to specialist, quality lead, team manager, and operations leader reduces the pull toward external opportunities.
• Engagement Culture: Long-standing program environments benefit from Cognizant's established recognition frameworks and financial services BPO community structures.

Attrition Risk Management Framework for the FIS Engagement
• Predictive Analytics: Machine learning-driven attrition risk scoring across the active workforce enabling early identification and intervention before attrition occurs.
• Career Pathing: Defined progression tracks within the FIS program including specialist, quality, and leadership pathways.
• Compensation Design: Tenure-linked bonuses, performance pay, and recognition structures aligned to program stability objectives.
• Knowledge Management: Cross-training, structured documentation, and knowledge transfer protocols that protect SLA continuity irrespective of individual attrition events.
• Manager Effectiveness: Manager performance scores correlated with team attrition data, driving proactive people management accountability at team level.

Cognizant is prepared to share program-level attrition data from comparable long-tenured managed services accounts under NDA, giving FIS evidence-based confidence in the stability of the dedicated TMS delivery team beyond what enterprise-level figures alone convey.

What is your average agent tenure overall? Break out by primary function where available: Customer Service, Fraud Operations, Disputes & Chargebacks, Debt & Collections, Back Office, and Risk & Compliance.

Cognizant’s financial services contact center operations are supported by a stable and experienced workforce across core functional areas. The average agent tenure (experience in role) across Fraud Operations, Disputes & Chargebacks, Debt & Collections, Back Office, and Risk & Compliance functions is approximately 2.5–3 years. At the frontline management and leadership level, average tenure ranges between 6–8 years, ensuring continuity, domain depth, and strong operational governance.

A functional‑level breakdown of average agent tenure is as follows:
● Customer Service: ~2.5–3 years
● Back Office Operations: ~2.5–3 years
● Disputes & Chargebacks: ~3–3.5 years
● Fraud Operations: ~2.5–3 years
● Debt & Collections: ~2.5–3 years
● Risk & Compliance: ~2–2.5 years

This tenure profile reflects Cognizant’s ability to retain experienced agents across regulated financial services operations while maintaining a strong pipeline of trained talent supported by structured onboarding, continuous learning, and role‑based career progression frameworks.
Optional. Are there any industry-relevant certifications, training frameworks, or accreditations held by your workforce that are applicable to financial services contact center operations?

Yes. Cognizant’s workforce holds multiple industry‑relevant certifications and training accreditations directly applicable to financial services contact center and operations delivery. These credentials support regulatory compliance, fraud and risk management, payments operations, and continuous service quality improvement.
Key certifications and training frameworks held by Cognizant’s workforce include:
● ISO 20022 Certification: Cognizant has 45+ workforce members certified in ISO 20022, enabling effective handling of payment inquiries, reconciliation, exception processing, and investigation of transaction anomalies through a strong understanding of payment message flows.
●GDPR / UK GDPR Compliance Training: Cognizant mandates GDPR and UK GDPR training for associates supporting regulated EMEA engagements. 1,000+ workforce members have completed this training, ensuring compliant handling of customer data and adherence to privacy regulations in contact center and back‑office operations.
● ACAMS (Anti‑Money Laundering) Certification: Approximately 20 workforce members hold ACAMS certification, strengthening fraud investigation, AML compliance, regulatory awareness, and case‑management rigor across financial services operations.
● Six Sigma / Lean Continuous Improvement Framework: All associates joining Cognizant’s Banking and Financial Services operations undergo Six Sigma White Belt training, followed by Yellow Belt certification as part of tenure progression. Green Belt training is provided to frontline leaders to drive SLA improvement, AHT reduction, quality enhancement, and throughput optimization through structured process improvement methodologies.

Together, these certifications and training frameworks ensure Cognizant’s contact center workforce is regulatory‑ready, quality‑driven, and equipped to support complex financial services operations at scale.

Provide three case studies and three client references on comparable engagements. Case studies should reflect large-scale, regulated financial services BPO. At least one must involve a migration of 300+ FTE.

Select engagements that reflect large-scale, regulated financial services BPO. At least one must involve a migration of 300+ FTE. Include one engagement where challenges were encountered — describe what happened and how they were resolved.
Financial Data & Analytics – Regulated Financial Services Provider

● Originations: Tax & Flood Services, CondoSafe (CoreLogic Product for Condominium U/W support)
● Servicing & Special Servicing: Loan Board & Search, File Maintenance & Data integrity, Research, escrow Tax Admin, HAMP/HARP/Loan Mod, Loss Mitigation support, Audit compliance Services
● Support Services: F&A, Legal, Doc management, Enterprise Data Services, Title & Rental Property services, Condosafe Operations, Transaction Data Entry

● Process Excellence & Lean Interventions
● AI / ML‑Driven Automation (automation, OCR, analytics, decision frameworks)
● Process Re‑engineering & Standardization
● Integrated Cognitive Technology & Digital Tooling

● 100% regulatory compliance and meticulous SLA adherence across mission critical operations. 
●  Processes ~$150B+ in payments annually across regulated, fraud sensitive environments. 
● +35% efficiency gains delivered within the first three years post acquisition. 
●  $20M+ in hard dollar benefits realized through digital transformation initiatives. 
● Demonstrated strong execution capability, consistently meeting or exceeding client stakeholder expectations and receiving multiple accolades for transition rigor and program management.

Challenges: 
Cognizant has been the strategic vendor partner, since the acquisition of its captive offshore operations.
● Data quality is a critical success factor of the client’s product and service offerings
● Extraction of relevant data points from multiple document formats from ~50Mn+ docs and 200Mn+ pages annually. 
● Improve accuracy: Expectation of >99.5% accuracy
● Cost control: Bring down the TCO 

Resolution:
● Executed large‑scale M&A‑driven transitions with seamless rebadging, strong offer acceptance, and post‑integration operational stability.
● Enabled seamless rebadging and post‑acquisition stabilization, ensuring uninterrupted delivery of mission‑critical operations through integrated people, platforms, and controls.
● Took over an 8‑year‑old mature low‑cost center and transformed operations across service lines within 36 months through right‑sizing and technology enablement.
● Delivered transformation within strict end‑client, regulatory, and platform constraints, reducing costs while enabling a scalable platform for profitable growth.
● Leveraged a BPaaS‑led operating model, combining hosted platforms, upfront process re‑engineering, scalable processing, and cognitive technologies to drive enterprise‑scale execution.
● Delivered 15+ technology and tool interventions and 8+ high‑impact Six Sigma initiatives, driving operational excellence and standardization.
● Implemented innovative commercial structuring through a robust ARC/RRC‑based TBP model, including amortized one‑time technology and transition costs.
● Optimized vendor portfolio to de‑risk dependency on Tier‑2 vendors and proactively address engagement risk factors.
Banking & Financial Services – Regulated Financial Institution

● Credit and Debit Fraud
● Credit and Debit disputes
● Deposit Claims
● Enterprise claims underwriting
● Digital Marketing 
● Currency threshold reporting
● Support services
● ACH Collection & claims
● Credit Bureau report

● Process Excellence & Lean (PEX) Interventions
● AI / Analytics‑Led Decisioning & Automation
● Process Re‑engineering & Standardization
● Integrated Cognitive Technology & Digital Tooling

● Improvement of denial rate from 24% to 31% through improved FTR%
● Cost savings through process improvements
● Reduction in write‑offs through multi‑transaction breakup and targeted recovery
●All KPIs met with zero SLA breaches, demonstrating consistent and predictable service delivery
● Mitigated losses through reducing low$ write off thresholds from $50 to $25

Challenges: 
● Migration of 300+ FTEs while sustaining BAU fraud dispute operations
● Lower denial rates and higher approval rates, leading to increased financial losses
● Complex and multi‑transaction dispute scenarios across multiple fraud types
● Difficulty in handling high‑complexity, multi‑transaction cases at scale
● High volume variability, requiring flexible and scalable staffing models
● Need for accurate fraud validation and evidence‑based decisioning to reduce leakage
● Pressure to reduce operational costs while improving dispute resolution outcomes
● Strong focus on recovering funds from card member‑initiated fraud disputes across diverse fraud categories

Resolution:
● Redesigned the end‑to‑end dispute lifecycle, standardizing processes and decision checkpoints
● Phased FTE transition with structured KT, parallel runs, and multi‑transaction disaggregation enabling focused investigations and recovery
● Established robust investigation frameworks, with complete case notes and defensible decisions
● Leveraged LexisNexis and digital research tools to strengthen fraud validation and denial accuracy
● Applied Lean / PEX interventions to reduce rework, improve quality, and drive efficiencies
● Deployed analytics dashboards and redefined KPIs for real‑time visibility and governance
● Enabled a scalable offshore delivery model ensuring consistent SLA performance
Capital Markets – Transfer Agent / Regulated Financial Services Provider

● Transfer transactions
● Trade execution
● Escheatment
● Dividend reinvestments
● Tax reporting
● Proxy solicitation
● Account maintenance

● CSAT improvement by 8% as customers were able to get through to agents
● 90% reduction in escheatment errors, impacting a working capital requirements by over 99%
● Executive escalations reduced by 100%
● Financial losses due to Operational errors reduced by 99%
● Fraud deduction errors reduced by 99%
● 3% Efficiency and productivity improvement improved (on a baseline of 95%)

Challenges: 
● Complex processes for customers and agents long processing times
● Antiquated technology 
● Monitor and improve call agent performance and end user experience on a continual basis and moving away from manual process.

Resolution:
● Implementation of full authentication over IVR/Chat BOT resulting in 10-12% effort reduction in potential fraud leading to operational losses
● Implementation of Self Help leading to improvement of IVR containment rate & ~20-25% reduction in call volume
● Implemented Speech Analytics tool
● Agent Segmentation using call behavior metrics from converted speech for actionable and focused attention for CSAT improvement.
● Profiling customers into categories using former output.

References must be contactable and from current or former financial services clients on comparable engagements. At least one reference should be able to speak to a transformation or large-scale migration program.

● Name: Kevin Tang
● Designation: VP, Strategic Deals and Vendor Management

Enterprise‑scale BPaaS and data operations transformation for a regulated financial data and analytics provider, including document‑intensive processing, AI/ML‑driven automation, process re‑engineering, platform‑led delivery, and large‑scale operations modernization across lending and property data services.
All communication will be facilitated through the Cognizant point of contact

Long‑term multi‑LoB engagement delivering high‑volume fraud and dispute operations for a leading banking client, enabled by global delivery, Lean‑led transformation, analytics‑driven controls, and sustained cost and regulatory outcomes.
All communication will be facilitated through Vamshi Rompikuntla, the Cognizant point of contact, who will coordinate with US Bank for the discussions.

For each commitment below, state your position clearly. Where you cannot fully commit, describe the conditions or constraints that apply.
Confirm you will provide open APIs with no data lock-in and full interoperability.
The proposal scope is moving the contact center applications and redesigning to suite CCaaS deployment and onboard agents  into NICE CCaaS owned by FIS
Confirm the FIS/TSYS retains full ownership of all data at all times, including during and after transition.

As part of the target operating model, all contact center agents will be onboarded onto the FIS‑owned NICE CCaaS platform. The NICE CCaaS environment, including all underlying infrastructure, data stores, and system access controls, will remain fully owned and governed by FIS.
Cognizant will not have access to customer system‑of‑record data or enterprise master data. Cognizant’s scope is strictly limited to the system‑of‑engagement layer, focused on enabling and hosting front‑office agent interactions within the FIS‑owned NICE CCaaS platform. Agents operating on this platform will handle customer interaction data only (e.g., voice, chat, interaction metadata) as required to service customer engagements, without direct access to core customer records or downstream systems of record.
Confirm no proprietary data formats will be used — all data exportable in standard formats at any time.

As part of the target operating model, all contact center agents will be onboarded onto the FIS‑owned NICE CCaaS platform. The NICE CCaaS environment, including all underlying infrastructure, data stores, and system access controls, will remain fully owned and governed by FIS. Cognizant does not own, control, or define data formats within the NICE CCaaS platform. The NICE CCaaS environment is cloud‑hosted and fully owned and governed by FIS, and all data structures, storage mechanisms, and export capabilities are governed by the native NICE CCaaS platform standards as configured by FIS.
Accordingly, Cognizant does not introduce or enforce any proprietary data formats. Any data exportability, format standards, and data access mechanisms are inherent platform capabilities of NICE CCaaS and remain under FIS ownership and control.
Confirm full data extraction rights upon contract exit, at no additional cost to the client.

Cognizant does not own, control, or govern data extraction rights or associated commercial terms. The NICE CCaaS platform is cloud‑hosted and fully owned by FIS, and all data access, retention, and extraction rights—including those applicable upon contract exit—are governed by FIS’s agreement with NICE and the platform’s native capabilities.
Cognizant does not impose any additional fees, proprietary mechanisms, or constraints related to data extraction. Any data extraction rights, formats, timelines, and associated costs at contract exit are defined and executed under FIS ownership and NICE CCaaS standard platform provisions.
Confirm you can provide a side-by-side coexistence environment for a minimum 12-month dual-run period.

An interim coexistence approach will be adopted during the migration from the legacy platform to NICE CCaaS, leveraging external voice transfers between platforms.
While this approach enables phased migration, it may have limitations on end‑to‑end contextualization across platforms. Additionally, any incremental telephony transfer–related costs arising from this interim setup are not included in scope, as such costs are dependent on existing carrier contracts held by TSYS and FIS.
Confirm you will fund technical debt remediation where your technology introduction creates new technical debt.

Cognizant is not introducing any new technology platforms as part of this engagement for the scope of migration related to front‑office agent interactions from legacy platforms to the FIS‑owned NICE CCaaS platform, which serves exclusively as a system of engagement.
NICE CCaaS is not positioned or implemented as a CRM or case management system, and Cognizant does not extend or repurpose the platform beyond its intended system‑of‑engagement capabilities. As a result, Cognizant does not create new technical debt through technology introduction.
Any remediation related to legacy technical debt or platform‑specific considerations remains outside Cognizant’s scope, unless explicitly defined and mutually agreed as part of a separate engagement. However, should the engagement evolve into a technology‑takeover and transformation program, Cognizant would adopt a phased approach with a dual‑run strategy. This would allow us to progressively look for any technical debt while adopting dual run strategy for the scope of front-office transformation by applying our expertise—once we gain full visibility into the existing licensing constructs, contractual constraints, and commercial models.
Confirm you will provide AI governance documentation covering bias monitoring, model drift, and explainability for all AI tools deployed.

Cognizant’s scope is limited to AI usage at the system‑of‑engagement layer only, in support of onboarding front‑office agents onto the FIS‑owned NICE CCaaS platform. Any AI‑enabled capabilities leveraged for front-office as part of this engagement are native to the NICE CCaaS platform or the FIS‑owned AWS environment, and are governed by FIS platform policies, controls, and governance frameworks.
For Front-Office , Cognizant is aligning to client defined existing stack of NICE and Microsoft and hence not introducing or deploying any standalone or additional AI tools as part of this engagement. Accordingly, Cognizant does not own or govern AI models, training data, bias monitoring, model drift management, or explainability mechanisms. AI governance documentation—including bias, drift, and explainability—remains under FIS ownership and the respective platform providers’ standard governance artifacts.
Confirm AI overlays can be integrated via API without requiring migration of core telephony or processing platforms.
Cognizant does not control platform‑level AI overlay extensibility. Any AI overlay integration capabilities are governed by NICE CCaaS platform features and FIS architectural decisions.
Confirm the client retains ownership of telephony infrastructure and IVR design unless explicitly agreed otherwise.

This assumption underpins all previous responses, and the technology selection is fully aligned to the NICE CCaaS platform as explicitly requested. Cognizant’s scope does not include onboarding or integrating any additional IVR platforms or telephony infrastructure beyond NICE CCaaS.
Confirm a full coexistence architecture plan will be provided prior to go-live, covering the full transition period.

We confirm that a full side‑by‑side coexistence environment for a minimum 12‑month dual‑run period is not envisaged. An interim coexistence approach will be adopted during the migration from the legacy platform to NICE CCaaS, leveraging external voice transfers between platforms.
This approach may introduce limitations in end‑to‑end contextualization and could result in additional telephony transfer–related costs. Such costs are not included in scope, as they are dependent on existing carrier contracts held by TSYS and FIS, which are currently not known to Cognizant.
Cognizant is not undertaking legacy platform takeover and does not have visibility into the contractual, commercial, or carrier models of the existing legacy environment. Accordingly, this response is based on current assumptions and may need to be refined post‑discovery, once legacy platform details and contractual constructs are fully understood.
Confirm all tooling (including AI Overlays) will be pre approved by the FIS/TSYS Governance team, comprised of architecture, AI Governance and Security Operation, prior to implementation.

Cognizant is not introducing or owning AI models. AI capabilities used are native to FIS‑owned NICE CCaaS or FIS‑owned AWS environments and are governed by FIS platform policies. AI governance artifacts (bias, drift, explainability) remain under FIS and platform‑provider ownership.

For each requirement listed in Column C, provide your response in Column D. Responses should be specific to this engagement and the regulatory environments in scope. Where certifications or formal frameworks apply, state them.
Vendor Response — How Your Solution Meets This Requirement
Disclose all sub-contractors by name, location, and scope. Confirm SLA flow-down and FIS audit rights over all 4th parties.

At this stage of the engagement, Cognizant does not propose the use of any subcontractors for the delivery of services in scope of the FIS Managed Services engagement. All services are intended to be delivered by Cognizant employees operating from Cognizant‑controlled delivery locations.
Should the use of any subcontractors become necessary in the future (e.g., for specialized or ancillary services), Cognizant will:

● Disclose all subcontractors in advance, including legal entity name, delivery location(s), and scope of services
● Obtain prior written approval from FIS before onboarding any subcontractor
● Remain fully accountable for subcontractor performance
Describe your internal governance model for monitoring sub-contractor performance and compliance.

Cognizant's sub-contractor governance model operates on multiple tiers: a universal Supplier Standards baseline applied to all vendors, a heightened risk-based oversight program for high-risk sectors like healthcare, annual audit and attestation cycles, mandatory incident reporting, and board-level compliance oversight. The model is notably cascade-based — obligations flow from Cognizant down through first-tier suppliers to their own subcontractors.
Confirm cardholder data will not leave approved jurisdictions without explicit client consent. Describe data residency controls.

Cognizant follows PCI DSS requirements for accounts where Cognizant access or impacts the security of cardholder data that falls under the scope of PCI. Not all areas of Cognizant are subject to PCI. We have external PCI assessments performed on the accounts that require it.
Describe the regulatory notification process before offshoring commences (e.g. FCA Principal 11 notification — UK; WARN Act — US). Confirm who is responsible for filings.

United Kingdom (UK).
For UK regulated activities, the Client will notify the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) of any material outsourcing or offshoring arrangement in accordance with the FCA Handbook (including Principle 11 – Relations with regulators, which requires firms to deal with the FCA in an open and cooperative way and to disclose appropriately anything of which the FCA would reasonably expect notice). Notifications cover the scope of services, locations, risks, controls, and governance for the proposed offshoring. [fca.org.uk], [burfordsolutions.com]
United States (US).
Where offshoring results in qualifying workforce reductions at a U.S. site, the Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) Act requires advance written notice (generally 60 days) to affected employees (or their representatives), the State dislocated worker unit, and the relevant local government. Responsibility for WARN compliance rests with the employer.
Responsibility for filings.
•	Client responsibility: The Client (as the regulated entity/employer) is responsible for determining applicability and making all regulatory and statutory filings/notifications (e.g., FCA notifications; WARN notices), including content, timing, and submission.
•	Service Provider support: The Service Provider will support the Client’s notifications by providing timely information, risk assessments, control descriptions, and implementation plans relevant to the offshoring, but does not file on the Client’s behalf unless expressly agreed in writing.
No commencement without clearance.
Offshoring will not commence until required notifications are completed, any mandatory approval periods have elapsed, and the Client has confirmed readiness to proceed.
Describe your approach to clients with explicit offshore restrictions in their current TMS contracts.

Contract‑first compliance.
Where a client’s existing TMS or master agreement includes explicit offshore restrictions, we treat those restrictions as binding delivery constraints. No services, data access, or support activities are moved offshore unless the contract is formally amended and the client provides prior written approval.

Ongoing governance and assurance.
Compliance with offshore restrictions is monitored through:

Contract Lifecycle Risk Management (CLRM) controls and periodic reviews.
Delivery governance, audits, and client reporting aligned to the contract’s notice and audit provisions.
Immediate escalation if any potential deviation risk is identified.
GDPR — confirm DPA agreements, data processor obligations, cross-border transfer mechanisms, and breach notification procedures (including SLA to notify FIS).

Cognizant typically acts as a data processor in respect of client personal data processed as part of Cognizant’s services. Cognizant will abide by its contractual obligations to the client and all applicable laws relating to data processors when processing the client’s personal data. Cognizant will only process the personal data in accordance with the client’s lawful written instructions and its contractual obligations. Furthermore, Cognizant will ensure compliance with any requirements with respect to disclosing personal data to sub-processors and will work with the client to implement an appropriate transfer mechanism before client personal data is transferred to a third country outside the European Economic Area.
US cardholder data — confirm CCPA compliance and your approach to state-level privacy law variation.

Cognizant has adopted and implemented a global privacy policy, which is an organization wide policy designed to promote the privacy, confidentiality, integrity, and security of personal data in compliance with applicable data privacy laws. Cognizant promotes compliance with this policy, as well as privacy laws and regulations applicable to Cognizant’s organization, through the oversight of its Privacy Team and Cognizant personnel charged with overseeing specific privacy-related matters, as well as its external advisors.
PCI DSS — confirm current certification level, scope, and most recent assessment date.

Cognizant is an ISO 27001:2022 certified organization with its information 
security structure based on ISMS (Information Security Management System) framework. Cognizant engages one of the world’s leading certification bodies for ISO 27001 audits. ISO 27001 Surveillance audits are conducted annually and a recertification is done once in 3 years.
PCI compliance audits are conducted for several of Cognizant’s Clients. If the project/account process, store and transmit card data as a service to the client and if client mandates for the account/project to be compliant with PCI DSS standard then account/project will certify for PCI DSS.
Confirm FIS retains full ownership and portability of all data at all times. Describe the mechanism for full data extraction upon contract exit.

Cognizant confirms that FIS retains full ownership of all customer and business data at all times. Cognizant acts solely as a data processor/custodian, handling data strictly in accordance with contractual obligations, client instructions, and applicable data protection requirements. Upon contract termination or exit, Cognizant supports complete data extraction, secure return or certified deletion of data, as contractually agreed, ensuring full data portability and no retention of client data beyond authorized timelines.
Confirm logical and/or physical segregation of cardholder data across different FIS clients.

Cognizant enforces strict logical and where contractually required, physical segregation of client. Logical segregation is implemented through client specific access controls, role based access, network segmentation (including dedicated VLANs), and environment level isolation, with access granted strictly on a need to know basis. Where mandated, physical segregation is achieved through dedicated Offshore Development Centers (ODCs) or isolated enclosures with restricted physical access. These controls collectively prevent cross client data commingling and unauthorized access.
Describe security controls for remote/WFH agents handling cardholder data (screen capture, device management, network security, print restrictions).

Cognizant implements robust security controls for remote and work from home agents handling sensitive customer data. These include the use of Cognizant managed and hardened endpoints, centralized device management, strong authentication and secure remote connectivity. Controls are in place to restrict or disable screen capture, removable media usage and local printing based on role & data sensitivity.
Who bears liability for regulatory fines resulting from vendor non-compliance? Describe indemnity and remediation framework for client disputes arising from vendor error.

Cognizant accepts responsibility for regulatory fines and penalties that arise directly and primarily from Cognizant’s non-compliance with applicable laws, regulations, or contractually assigned obligations, to the extent such obligations are under our control. This allocation of liability is subject to the mutually agreed limitations of liability, exclusions, and carve-outs defined in the Master Services Agreement.
Cognizant provides standard indemnification to protect the client against third-party claims, losses, damages, and costs, in accordance with the indemnity provisions outlined in the Master Services Agreement. In the event of an issue attributable to Cognizant, we follow a structured remediation and governance process to address the incident, mitigate its impact, and prevent recurrence
CFPB — describe your compliance program and oversight model for collections, disputes, and customer service operations.

Compliance program for collections, disputes, and customer service operations is structured and supported by a comprehensive quality assurance framework endorsed by The Association of Credit and Collections Professionals (ACA) . This framework governs operations through documented policies, mandatory training, internal audits, corrective action processes, and continuous improvement initiatives. Oversight is provided through a dedicated Compliance Officer and Regulatory Attorney, ensuring adherence to federal and state consumer protection laws, prompt remediation of issues, and continuous enhancement of compliance controls
FDCPA / TCPA — describe compliance approach for collections operations in the United States.

FDCPA and TCPA compliance is designed and implemented in alignment with CFPB supervision and examination expectations. The program incorporates a formal Compliance Management System, documented policies and procedures, mandatory training, monitoring and testing, complaint and dispute management, corrective action, and independent oversight. Compliance effectiveness is routinely evaluated through audits, management reviews, and third party assessments consistent with CFPB examination frameworks
FCA regulated activities — describe your compliance approach for UK-regulated cardholder servicing including complaint handling.
Cognizant relies on the client's policies and procedures and proceeds with handling according to approved guidance.
Germany (BaFin) and Netherlands (AFM/DNB) — describe relevant considerations for Hamburg and Barneveld operations.

Cognizant aligns with the supervisory expectations of BaFin in Germany and AFM/DNB in the Netherlands. We emphasize compliance with BaFin’s IT security and outsourcing requirements, ensuring robust governance and audit readiness. And in Barneveld, we focus on AFM’s conduct standards and DNB’s prudential oversight, with strong vendor management, certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2), and transparent reporting. Across both jurisdictions, we maintain strict adherence to EU directives of regulatory compliance and operational resilience.
Add any further regulatory requirements, jurisdictional constraints, or compliance considerations relevant to your proposed delivery model that are not covered above.
No additional regulatory requirements, jurisdictional constraints, or compliance considerations are applicable beyond those already addressed above.
